# Hydra-Bench

`hydra-bench` holds the synthetic inference benchmark workloads used to compare
inference performance across Hydra hosts. The modules under `hydra.bench.*` are
**not** part of the runtime kernel and are **not** regenerated by the default
sync pipeline — they're deliberately stress-shaped (deep curried cascades, wide
records, branchy DAGs) and would balloon codegen time on slower hosts.

Use this package by running `bin/sync-bench.sh` to regenerate the bench
workloads for a chosen set of hosts, then `bin/run-inference-bench.sh` to drive
the actual measurement.

## What it provides

- **`hydra.bench.linearChain`** — depth-N monomorphic chain of `walker0` …
  `walker399`, each cases on `_Term` variants and recurses to the previous
  walker. Stresses depth-of-recursion in type inference.
- **`hydra.bench.polymorphicChain`** — depth-N chain with `forall`-typed
  signatures. Stresses type-variable substitution and unification.
- **`hydra.bench.fanOut`** — branchy DAG with many fan-out points. Stresses
  graph-shape codegen and dependency resolution.

## Workflow

```bash
# 1. Regenerate the bench modules for the hosts you'll measure.
bin/sync-bench.sh --hosts haskell,java,python

# 2. Run the benchmark.
bin/run-inference-bench.sh --series all
```

## Why a separate package

These modules are deliberately pathological in size and shape. Embedding them
in `hydra-kernel` made every host's regen step pay the cost of translating
17.5k+ lines of generated walker functions across 7+ target languages — fast
for hosts with linear-time coders but, on the Lisp dialects, slow enough to
trip CI runner watchdogs. Splitting them into their own opt-in package keeps
the default `bin/sync.sh` lean while preserving the cross-host comparison
property.

## Dependencies

- `hydra-kernel` (for the term/type model referenced by the bench namespaces)
